original_hypothesis.title: PermissionsRegistry.removeRoleFrom Loop Fails to Break After Swap-Pop Causing Double Removal
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: In removeRoleFrom, the swap-and-pop loops for both _roleToAddresses and _addressToRoles do not break after finding and removing the target element. If the swapped-in last element happens to match the search condition, the loop will pop it too, removing TWO entries instead of one. This corrupts the permission tracking arrays and could cause addresses to lose role assignments they should retain.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: The function iterates over arrays, performs swap-and-pop when it finds a match, but continues iterating. After the swap, the last element is now at position i. If the next iteration checks this swapped element and it matches (which can happen in _addressToRoles if the user has duplicate-keyed roles from prior corruption via removeRole bug), a second pop occurs.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Two swap-and-pop loops without break statements. If swapped-in element matches condition, double-pop occurs: rta[i] = rta[rta.length-1]; rta.pop(); and loop continues checking position i again.

preconditions: The last element in _roleToAddresses or _addressToRoles matches the removal target after swap
Prior removeRole corruption has created duplicate entries in _addressToRoles

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: After swap: rta[i] = rta[length-1]; rta.pop(). If rta[i] (now the swapped element) == target, next iteration pops again. In _roleToAddresses, if last address == c, double-removal occurs.
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Admin removing a role from an address inadvertently removes another address's role assignment
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Corrupted permissions could allow unauthorized access or deny legitimate access to critical protocol functions

economic_questions: What is the probability of the swapped element matching the search condition?
Which roles control financial operations (GAUGE_ADMIN, GOVERNANCE)?
Could corrupted permissions enable unauthorized gauge creation or fee changes?
validation_requirements: Create scenario: two addresses with same role, remove role from first where second is last in array
Verify second address also loses role assignment
Assess impact on downstream contracts checking hasRole

market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Could affect protocol governance and access control

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: Addresses losing role assignments they should retain
potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Loss of privileged access to protocol functions
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: Depends on which roles are corrupted

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Array ordering after multiple setRoleFor/removeRoleFrom calls
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Test double-removal scenario in unit test

validation_result.impact_summary: Filtered before validation: attack_realism is LOW
validation_result.detailed_analysis: This hypothesis was auto-rejected because its attack_realism grade is LOW. Preconditions require actions outside the attacker's control (off-chain compromise, victim cooperation, or admin collusion). No LLM validation was performed.
